name: Rule-Based Programming

corePrinciples: An engine matches and fires rules
x.controlDataFlow: Data-driven: an inference engine matches rule conditions against facts and fires actions, which update facts, until quiescence.
x.suitableProjects: Business-rule engines, expert systems, fraud / eligibility logic, and domains where rules change often and independently.

def: A paradigm that encodes logic as a set of independent condition→action rules evaluated by an inference engine against a fact base.
problem: Business logic that changes frequently and is authored by domain experts is brittle when buried in imperative code.
mechanism: Rules and facts are separated; a matching engine (often the Rete algorithm) finds satisfied rules, a conflict-resolution policy picks which to fire, and firing updates working memory — repeating until no rule applies.
benefits: Good for large, changing rule sets
limits: Emergent behaviour hard to predict / debug
Performance tuning of the match network
whenNot: Small, stable logic (a plain function is clearer)
myths: "Rules are always clearer than code" — large rule bases can be harder to reason about than procedures
